coredata.dc:title: Pectin/gelatin-based bioactive composite films reinforced with sulfur functionalized carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Ezati, Parya
Roy, Swarup
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Sulfur functionalized turmeric-derived carbon dots (S-CD) were prepared and used to fabricate pectin/gelatin-based bioactive food packaging film. FTIR and XPS analysis confirmed that turmeric-derived CDs were functionalized with sulfur. The sizes of CD and S-CD measured by TEM were 10.6 ± 1.2 nm and 8.5 ± 1.6 nm, respectively. Turmeric-derived CD showed strong antibacterial and antioxidant activity with insignificant cytotoxicity against mouse fibroblast L929 cells. The CD-added film showed high UV protection properties without significantly changing the transparency of the pectin/gelatin film. The incorporation of CD also affected the mechanical, water vapor permeability, and hydrophobicity of the film. In addition, CD-added pectin/gelatin-based films showed strong antioxidant activity as determined by the DPPH and ABTS methods. Also, the film with sulfur functionalized CD showed strong antimicrobial activity against food-borne pathogenic bacteria, E. coli and L. monocytogenes. The sulfur functionalized turmeric-derived carbon dots (S-CD) incorporated pectin/gelatin films with superior functional properties can be used in active food packaging applications to ensure food safety and extend the shelf life of foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Sulfur functionalization
Pectin/gelatin
Antimicrobial
Antioxidant
Active packaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Appearance and optical properties of the film
Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government ( MSIT ) (No. 2019R1A2C2084221 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The visual appearance and (b) light transmittance spectra of the pectin/gelatin-based films.
(a) FESEM cross-sectional images and (b) AFM micrographs of the pectin/gelatin-based films.
FTIR spectra of the pectin/gelatin-based films.
TGA and DTG thermograms of the pectin/gelatin-based films.
Antioxidant activity of the pectin/gelatin-based films determined by ABTS and DPPH methods.
Antibacterial activity of the pectin/gelatin-based films against L. monocytogenes and E. coli.
Schematic representation for the preparation of carbon dot incorporated pectin/gelatin-based film.
Surface color and light transmittance of the pectin/gelatin-based composite films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The values are presented as a mean ± standard deviation. Different letters within the same column indicate significant differences (p < 0.05). L, a, b: Hunter Lab values; ∆E: total color difference, WI; whiteness index; YI: yellowness index; T280 and T660: light transmittance at 280 and 660 nm, respectively .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mechanical properties, water vapor permeability (WVP), and water contact angle (WCA) of the pectin/gelatin-based composite films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The values are presented as a mean ± standard deviation. Different letters within the same column indicate significant differences (p < 0.05). TS: tensile strength; EB: elongation at break; EM: elastic modulus; WVP: water vapor permeability; WCA: water contact angle.

head.ce:title: Pectin/gelatin-based bioactive composite films reinforced with sulfur functionalized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfur functionalized turmeric-derived carbon dots (S-CD) were prepared and used to fabricate pectin/gelatin-based bioactive food packaging film. FTIR and XPS analysis confirmed that turmeric-derived CDs were functionalized with sulfur. The sizes of CD and S-CD measured by TEM were 10.6 ± 1.2 nm and 8.5 ± 1.6 nm, respectively. Turmeric-derived CD showed strong antibacterial and antioxidant activity with insignificant cytotoxicity against mouse fibroblast L929 cells. The CD-added film showed high UV protection properties without significantly changing the transparency of the pectin/gelatin film. The incorporation of CD also affected the mechanical, water vapor permeability, and hydrophobicity of the film. In addition, CD-added pectin/gelatin-based films showed strong antioxidant activity as determined by the DPPH and ABTS methods. Also, the film with sulfur functionalized CD showed strong antimicrobial activity against food-borne pathogenic bacteria, E. coli and L. monocytogenes. The sulfur functionalized turmeric-derived carbon dots (S-CD) incorporated pectin/gelatin films with superior functional properties can be used in active food packaging applications to ensure food safety and extend the shelf life of foods.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, plastics are widely used in packaging materials because of their excellent properties, ease of use, and economy. However, most plastic packaging materials are single-use and do not naturally biodegrade when discarded, causing many environmental problems [1]. Recently, biopolymer-based packaging materials have emerged as a safer alternative to synthetic plastics due to their biodegradability, renewability, environmentally-friendliness, and sustainability. To this end, various polysaccharides and proteins derived from plants and animals have been used alone or mixed to manufacture biodegradable packaging materials [2].

Pectin is one of the polysaccharide-based biopolymers of interest because of its excellent film-forming properties, which are abundant and cost-effective [3]. Pectin is a complex polysaccharide composed primarily of linear homopolymers of α-1,4-linked galacturonic acids commonly found in the cell walls of plants [4]. Gelatin, one of the protein-based polymers, is a protein widely used to manufacture biopolymer films. It is a glutinous protein mainly obtained by denaturing and hydrolyzing collagen from animal tissues [5]. Both pectin and gelatin are edible biopolymers and are commonly used as thickening or gelling agents in the food industry. Pectin and gelatin are also known to have good film-forming properties, and their combination has been reported to produce compatible films with excellent film properties [6]. For the development of value-added packaging films, polymers are usually fortified with functional fillers to enhance their physicochemical properties with additional functions such as antibacterial, antioxidant, and UV protection properties [7]. As functional fillers, nanomaterials are attracting great attention because they improve the physical and functional properties of composite films [8].

Various nanofillers have been used to prepare biopolymer-based nanocomposite films [9]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs), carbonaceous nanomaterials with dimensions of less than 10 nm, have emerged as potential materials for food packaging applications due to their enthralling properties such as water solubility, excellent compatibility, cost-effectiveness, good chemical stability, and low cytotoxicity [10]. In addition, the surface functional groups of CD exhibit excellent functional properties such as luminescence, UV protection, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties [11]. CDs can be made using various synthetic and natural carbon sources, with natural sources being particularly advantageous [12]. One of such natural sources for the preparation of CDs was turmeric [13–16]. Several research works have been carried out to prepare bionanocomposite films by adding CD [10,11,17]. However, work on surface-modified CDs such as sulfur functionalized CDs (S-CDs) to prepare biopolymer-based films for food packaging applications is still lacking.

Therefore, in this study, highly fluorescent CDs were prepared using green source turmeric powder as a precursor material and further functionalized with sulfur to enhance the functionality of CDs. The developed CD was used to prepare pectin/gelatin-based nanocomposite films. The mechanical, hydrodynamic, and thermal properties of the composite film were determined. In addition, the functional properties of the CD-added pectin/gelatin composite film, such as UV protection, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties, were evaluated.

CD was synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method using an aqueous solution of turmeric containing ammonium persulfate as a sulfur source ( Scheme 1). Briefly, 1 g of turmeric and 0.1 g of functionalizing formulation were dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water with stirring and placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) lined stainless steel reactor (100 mL) and reacted at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the reaction product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove unreacted materials. Then, the supernatant was filtered through a membrane filter (25 mm diameter nylon filter, pore size: 0.20 µm, Whatman International Ltd., Maidstone, England) to remove impurities. The filtrate was then lyophilized to obtain CD powder. For comparison, turmeric carbon dots (CD) were also prepared using the same procedure without a sulfur source. The prepared CDs were designated as CD and S-CD, and the details on the characterization and results are given in the Supporting information.

Pectin/gelatin-based films were prepared using a solution casting method, as shown in Scheme 1 [22,23]. For this, 2 g each of pectin and gelatin and 1.2 g of glycerol were dissolved in 150 mL of distilled water with vigorous stirring at 80 °C for 30 min. Then, 0.08 g of CD (2 wt% based on polymer) were added slowly to the solution with vigorous stirring for one hour. The film-forming solution was promptly cast onto a leveled Teflon film-coated glass plate (24 cm × 30 cm) and dried at 25 °C for two days. The dried film was peeled off the plate and conditioned at 25 °C and 50% RH for at least two days. For comparison, a neat pectin/gelatin film was prepared using the same procedure without CD addition. According to the CD type, the prepared films were designated pec/gel, pec/gel/CD, and pec/gel/S-CD. The methods for measuring film properties were described in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Appearance and optical properties of the film
ce:section.ce:para: The apparent image of the fabricated films is shown in Fig. 1 a. The control pectin/gelatin film had a pale yellow color due to the pectin, whereas the films with CD and S-CD added showed a bright dark yellow color due to the brown-yellow color of the carbon dots. The optical properties of bio-based composite films can vary depending on the interaction between the biopolymer and the functional material. The surface color and optical properties of the pectin/gelatin-based composite films are shown in Table 1. The addition of CD and S-CD slightly decreased the brightness (L-value) of the pectin/gelatin film while significantly increasing the yellowness (b-value) and slightly increasing the redness (a-value) of the pectin/gelatin film, which was due to the brownish-yellow color of the carbon dots. As the a and b values increased, the total color difference (∆E) of the composite films also increased significantly. Accordingly, notable changes were observed in other color characteristics such as whiteness (WI) and yellowness index (YI). Similar to the L-value, the addition of CD and S-CD slightly decreased the WI of the film, whereas the addition of CD and S-CD increased the YI of the film significantly due to the brownish-yellow color of the carbon dots. The increasing pattern of the YI was similar to the b-value of the composite films.

The light transmittance of the pectin/gelatin-based films was determined to evaluate the transparency and UV-barrier properties of the film (Fig. 1 b). The neat pectin/gelatin film was very transparent to visible light. It interestingly showed a low UV light (UVB: 280–320 nm) transmittance, which is attributed to the UV blocking properties of gelatin [5]. The addition of CD and S-CD slightly reduced the transparency of the film, i.e., slightly decreased the light transmittance to visible light. However, the addition of CDs almost completely prevented the UVB light transmittance. UV protection is one of the main functions of food packaging film to maintain food quality by inhibiting photooxidation of food. The transparency and UV-barrier properties of the film were evaluated by measuring the light transmittance at 660 nm (T660) and 280 nm (T280), respectively, and the results are shown in Table 1. The T660 of the film did not change significantly (p > 0.05) when CD was added but decreased significantly (p < 0.05) when S-CD was added due to the sulfur functionalization of CD. The T280 of the control film itself was low due to the UV-light barrier properties of the matrix polymer (pectin and gelatin) [6,18] which was reduced more by the addition of CD and S-CD. The CD-added composite film not only blocks UVB almost completely but also blocks UVA (320–400 nm) largely, so it can be usefully used for food packaging materials that are sensitive to UV rays. The UV blocking effect of the composite film is likely due to the UV absorbing properties of the CD [12]. A similar UV protection effect was reported for bacterial cellulose films containing carbon dots synthesized with lactic acid bacteria [19].

The FESEM cross-section images and AFM surface micrographs of the pectin/gelatin-based films are shown in Fig. 2. The FESEM image of the pectin/gelatin films (Fig. 2 a) showed a compact structure indicating good compatibility between pectin and gelatin, preferably due to the H-bonding between the COO- of the carbohydrate and -OH of the protein [20]. The CD and S-CD were well dispersed in the polymer matrix without significant morphological changes due to the hydrophilicity of CDS. However, the S-CD added film showed crystal structure (in higher resolution SEM image) probably due to the sulfur functionalization. As shown in the AFM surface images (Fig. 2 b), the CD and S-CD were evenly distributed throughout the film with no substantial change in the surface roughness of the film as confirmed by AFM analysis (Ra and Rq values). Microscopic observation has shown that hydrophilic CDs are highly compatible with pectin/gelatin-based polymer matrices. Similar morphologies have been reported for soy protein isolate-based films incorporating citric acid-derived amines and carboxyl functionalized carbon dots [21].

The mechanical properties of the pectin/gelatin-based films are shown in Table 2. The TS of the neat pectin/gelatin film was 55.3 MPa, indicating the composite was relatively strong, probably due to the excellent intermolecular interaction between pectin and gelatin via the -OH group of the polysaccharide (pectin) and the deprotonated carboxyl group (-COO-) of protein (gelatin) [25]. The addition of CD slightly enhanced the tensile strength (TS) due to the interfacial interaction between the CD and the biopolymer matrix. In contrast, the addition of S-CD reduced the TS of the film, which is most likely due to the sulfur functionalization of CD. Though the addition of S-CD significantly reduced the TS, the mechanical strength of the film still is strong enough (TS > 20 MPa) and comparable to the commercially used LDPE film for food packaging applications [26]. The addition of CD also had a distinct effect on the elongation at break (EB) and modulus of elasticity (EM). When CD was added, EB decreased, and EM increased, whereas S-CD showed the opposite trend, consistent with the generally observed trend of mechanical properties of composite films. The low TS and EM with the increased EB of the S-CD-added films may be due to the formation of crystalline structures due to sulfur functionalization as observed in the FESEM micrographs (Fig. 2). Similar to this result, it was reported that the addition of lactic acid bacteria synthesized carbon dots decreased TS and increased the EB of the cellulose-based film extensively [19]. The different effects of CD and S-CD on the mechanical properties of composite films may be due to the surface functional groups of the carbon dots. Similar effects of surface functional groups on mechanical properties have also been reported for citric acid-derived amine and carboxyl functionalized carbon dot added soy protein isolate-based films [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA)
ce:section.ce:para: The WVP and WCA of the pectin/gelatin-based films are also presented in Table 2. The WVP of the film decreased significantly (p < 0.05) by the addition of CD, but it was not significantly changed (p > 0.05) by the addition of S-CD. The increased water vapor barrier (i.e., decreased WVP) of the CD-added film may be due to the formation of a tortuous path of water vapor diffusion by the CD in the polymer matrix. However, the effect of preventing water vapor diffusion of the CD may be reduced by the surface hydrophilic functional groups of the S-CD. On the other hand, the WCA of the pectin/gelatin-based film was significantly reduced by adding CDs. The decrease of WCA by adding CD fillers was larger in the case of adding S-CD than in the case of CD, perhaps due to increased hydrophilic functional groups of S-CD.

The thermal stability of the pectin/gelatin-based films was evaluated using thermogravimetric analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. 4. All pectin/gelatin-based films showed multi-step weight changes. The first weight change occurred at 50–120 °C due to water evaporation [27]. The second thermal degradation was observed in the range of 125–250 °C with the maximum degradation at 210 °C, attributed to the breakdown of glycerol and low molecular weight proteins of gelatin [28]. The final thermal degradation started around 255 °C and ended at 400 °C, with a maximum of 315 °C, due to the decomposition of the pectin and high molecular weight proteins of gelatin [22]. There was a noticeable change in the thermal degradation pattern of the film with the addition of CD. In the first stage of the weight change, the CD-added films lost more slowly as the carbon dots entrapped the moisture. The CD-added films showed slightly different third step decomposition due to the hydrogen bonds with the biopolymers and surface functionalization of carbon dots. The final residual content of the film showed somewhat high values of 32–38%, possibly due to non-combustible minerals and impurities in the pectin and gelatin. The addition of CD increased the char content, especially S-CD, and increased the char content by ~5%, probably due to sulfur functionalization of the carbon dots.

The ABTS and DPPH free radical scavenging activity of the pectin/gelatin-based films is shown in Fig. 5. The antioxidant activity of the neat pectin/gelatin film was negligible, while the CD-added films showed excellent antioxidant activity. The addition of CD and S-CD increased the free radical scavenging capacity of the pectin/gelatin film to 96.6% and 90.1% in the ABTS method and 79.5% and 75.6% in the DPPH method, respectively. The higher antioxidant activity value of the CD-added film compared to the S-CD-added film is due to the presence of surface hydroxyl groups in the CD [29]. The H• transfer from the hydroxyl groups to the ABTS• and DPPH• radicals resulted in quenched forms of ABTS-H and DPPH-H [30]. Free radicals restore stability and bind together, preventing ABTS or DPPH free radical quenching. Similar antioxidant activity of CDs against DPPH free radicals has been reported [31]. It has been suggested that the high antioxidant activity of CD is due to the presence of numerous electron-donating groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [19]. In addition, the higher antioxidant activity values of pectin/gelatin-based films in the ABTS method than in the DPPH method are attributed to the better interaction between ABTS free radicals and hydrophilic CDs. The lower free radical scavenging ability of S-CD added film compared to CD added film is due to the generation of more OH- radicals [28,32].

The antibacterial activity of the pectin/gelatin-based films was evaluated using the total viable colony count method against food-borne pathogenic bacteria, L. monocytogenes and E. coli, and the results are shown in Fig. 6. As expected, the neat pectin/gelatin film did not show any antibacterial activity against both test bacteria, while CD-added films showed distinctive antibacterial activity. The CD-added film (Pec/Gel/CD film) reduced the growth of L. monocytogenes and E. coli by about 2 Log CFU/mL compared to the control group at the end of the 12 h exposure, which was due to the antibacterial activity of CD [33]. Although CD has broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity, its antimicrobial activity is highly dependent on various factors such as carbon source, particle size, morphology, manufacturing method, etc. Although the antibacterial activity of CD has not yet been elucidated, the most likely antibacterial activity of CD is the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). It is believed that ROS, such as O2- and OH- ions, accumulate on bacterial surfaces and increase membrane permeability, leading to oxidative stress and consequent cell death [19]. On the other hand, the S-CD-added film (Pec/Gel/S-CD film) exhibited strong antibacterial activity against both test bacteria, resulting in a decrease in growth by 5 Log CFU/mL compared to the control group after 12 h of exposure. The stronger antimicrobial activity of the S-CD-added film is probably due to specific functional groups such as sulfonates and sulfonic acids, which bind to the enzyme's -SH functional group and generate ROS stress that denatures proteins and lipids [34]. It is plausible to postulate that sulfur metabolites can react with cellular lipids to reduce total lipid content and release cellular components, thereby inhibiting the growth of microorganisms [35].

Food packaging materials must have low cytotoxicity, and high biocompatibility as the packaging film can contact the food, and its additives can migrate into the food [36]. Nanoparticles have been used to develop packaging films as antibacterial fillers; however, they can sometimes aggregate and degrade the properties of the films, especially if their affinity with the film is low. Besides antibacterial activity, another important property of food packaging film is its antioxidant activity [31,37,38]. Attempts have been made to impart antioxidant activity to packaging films using essential oils, natural plant extracts, or polyphenolic compounds [39–41]. This study manufactured a packaging film using a carbon dot (CD) with high hydrophilicity and excellent dispersibility in a polymer matrix. The CD was more effective with durable antioxidant and antibacterial activity as a multifunctional nanomaterial. In addition, CD showed negligible cytotoxicity against L929 mouse fibroblasts. Therefore, CDs can be used as multifunctional fillers in food packaging applications due to their excellent functional properties, high biocompatibility, and negligible toxicity. However, further studies on polymer matrices containing CD are needed to determine their performance in real food.

Sulfur functionalized carbon dots (S-CD) were synthesized using a facile hydrothermal method using turmeric as a carbon source with ammonium persulfate as sulfur sources. CDs were added to pectin/gelatin films to investigate their potential as functional fillers in food packaging films. The CDs were uniformly dispersed in the pectin/gelatin film, improving the UV blocking function without significantly affecting the transparency of the film. The addition of CD increased the mechanical properties and decreased water vapor permeability and water contact angle of the films. The S-CD-added pectin/gelatin film showed excellent antioxidant properties with strong antibacterial activity against food-borne pathogenic bacteria, E. coli and L. monocytogenes. Therefore, S-CD-added films with improved functional properties have great potential for value-added, low-cost, active packaging applications.
Experimental section: Carbon dots characterization, Film characterization, and properties.
Results: CDs characterizations, TEM and XPS (Fig. S1), Light absorption profile (Fig. S2), In vitro cytotoxicity test (Fig. S3), References.

Parya Ezati: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data Analysis, Writing – original draft. Swarup Roy: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data Analysis, Writing – original draft. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. 2019R1A2C2084221).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.128123.
